text: The color red has specific physiological effects: it elevates heart rate and signals urgency. Early smartphone engineers chose red for notification badges knowing this. The choice was not arbitrary — it was imported from traffic signals and emergency warnings, contexts where red reliably triggers an orienting response. This is notification design at the level of perceptual hijacking: before conscious thought engages, the visual system has already flagged the badge as a priority. Philosopher James Williams calls this 'capturing' attention — commandeering cognitive resources without consent. Changing notification badges to gray, as some digital wellness tools do, measurably reduces compulsive checking.
question: According to the passage, why was red specifically chosen for notification badges?

options.text: It was the easiest color for early smartphone displays to render accurately
It matches the branding colors used by most major social media platforms
It triggers an orienting response in the visual system before conscious thought engages
User testing showed red badges were clicked more often than other colors
